question: how far did market garden get
title: How Far Did My Market Garden Actually Get? A Real Backyard Story
metaDescription: What happens when a backyard gardener in Houston tries to sell at the farmers market? Here's the honest progress report on Scotty's market garden journey.
content:
# How Far Did My Market Garden Actually Get? A Real Backyard Story

I want to be straight with y'all about this. My market garden is not a success story in the traditional sense, not yet. It's a working story. A real-time experiment in whether a regular person in the middle of Houston can grow enough food in a backyard to justify a table at the farmers market and build something worth showing up to every week.

That question, how far did it get, is probably the most interesting one I get asked. Because the answer tells you what this whole thing is actually about.

## Where It Started

I started the market gardening idea because I had too much food. That's the honest origin. I was growing for myself across 16 raised beds, nine by three feet each, and the harvests were outpacing what I could eat, give away, or put up. I grow in living soil, using only compost as my amendment, no synthetic fertilizers, no chemicals, and the productivity of that biology genuinely surprised me.

So the natural question was: why not sell it?

But I wanted to do this publicly, on camera, as a way to show other people that it's possible. Not possible in some hypothetical sense, but actually doable in a Houston backyard, with real city soil, compost-based growing, and a real market. I wanted to document the whole thing, the wins and the hard parts, so other people could see what it actually looks like to try.

My initial target was Memorial Villages Farmers Market in Houston. I know Tim and Leanne, the folks who run it, and I talked with them about what the requirements look like for a grower like me. That first conversation confirmed there was a real path to getting a table there.

## The Gap Between Garden and Market

Here's what nobody tells you about going from backyard gardener to market gardener: the gap between growing a lot and growing enough to sell consistently is bigger than it looks.

Market gardening requires consistency. Your customers come back expecting what you had last week. You can't show up one Saturday with a gorgeous haul and the next Saturday with almost nothing because your timing was off or a heat wave hit. Consistency requires succession planting, starting fresh rounds of seeds every week or two so you always have something coming in, staggered across multiple varieties.

It also requires volume. A single farmers market table needs enough product to make the trip worthwhile, covering your booth fee, your time, your travel, and still putting money in your pocket. The economics only pencil out at a certain scale, and that scale requires more than 16 beds of mixed vegetables.

Research from Mother Earth News and the ATTRA program shows that a well-managed small market garden can generate real income. Some operations are pulling six figures from just a few acres. But those operations are optimized. They know exactly what sells. They've refined their crop selection to focus on what commands premium prices. They've built customer relationships. All of that takes time.

## What the Garden Actually Produced

My 16 beds in Houston produced real, beautiful food. Lettuces, arugula, herbs, tomatoes, beans, peppers. The quality of what comes out of biologically active, compost-fed soil is genuinely different from what you'd buy at a grocery store. The flavor is there. The nutritional density is there. Produce from living soil has a vitality that chemically produced food just doesn't have.

But I was honest with myself about the gaps. The volume wasn't quite there yet for a full weekly market table. And I was still working out the succession planting rhythm that keeps product flowing consistently through the season.

So the honest answer to how far it got is: far enough to prove the concept, not yet far enough to call it a functioning business. That's actually a meaningful distinction. Proof of concept is the hardest part. Once you know the soil works, the compost works, the biology works, the rest is iteration.

## What the Journey Actually Produced

Here's what I think the market garden produced that's more valuable than the produce itself. A model.

I want to inspire other people to become market gardeners. Not just because of the economics, though the economics can work, but because of what it means for how communities relate to food. When more people grow food and more people sell at farmers markets, the whole food culture of a community shifts. Customers start to understand where food comes from. They start to value the real cost of growing it. They start to demand better.

That's the movement I'm trying to start. Not just my own market garden, a wave of backyard growers showing up at farmers markets all over Houston, all over Texas, all over the country. People selling food grown in living soil, compost-amended, no chemical shortcuts. That's the alternative to industrial food.

One thing I tell people considering this path: look for produce that's not uniform. When a vendor at the market has all perfectly identical tomatoes, they're probably not growing them themselves. Real garden produce has variation, different sizes, different shapes, a range of maturity. That variation is a sign of honest growing. Be proud of it.

## The Path Forward

The market garden has not stopped. It's evolving. More succession planting, more attention to which crops move at market, more conversations with Tim and Leanne about timing and what their customers are actually buying.

The land work out in Neadville, the regenerative agriculture project, is producing compost that feeds back into the Houston garden. Picking up cabbage from produce marts, building hot piles, turning that organic matter into biology-rich compost, and applying it to the beds. That closed loop is what makes the whole operation possible without external inputs.

Scaling a market garden from concept to business is a multi-year project. The research backs this up, it typically takes two to four years for a new market garden to hit its stride in terms of crop selection, customer relationships, and operational efficiency. That timeline is humbling, but it's honest.

And honestly, the journey is the point. Every week I'm learning something about what grows best, what sells best, what the soil responds to. That knowledge doesn't come from a book. You build it by doing it, by being in the garden, by showing up at the market and talking to customers.

## Why This Story Matters

A hundred years ago, most families had a working relationship with food production. It was part of life in a way that's been pretty much completely severed by industrialization. Getting it back isn't going to happen through institutional change. It's going to happen through a thousand backyard gardeners deciding to take their excess harvest to market. Through a thousand tables at farmers markets selling food grown in real soil. Through a thousand conversations between growers and eaters about what food actually is and where it comes from.

That's how far the market garden has gotten. Not far enough yet. But far enough to know it's real, it's possible, and it's worth continuing.

## Sources

- Earning Potential of Market Gardening, Mother Earth News
- Market Gardening: A Start Up Guide, ATTRA Sustainable Agriculture
- How to Start Selling Market Garden Produce, Bootstrap Farmer
- Starting A Market Garden: The Complete Beginners Guide, GroCycle
- Market Garden, Wikipedia
